questions.question_text: A brokerage has both the buyer and seller as clients in the same transaction. What must the broker do to allow full representation of each party?
questions.correct_answer: Designate separate licensees for each party
distractors: Refer one party to a different brokerage
Reduce duties owed to both parties equally
Obtain a court order permitting dual service
questions.explanation: When both buyer and seller are clients of the same brokerage in Kansas, the broker may designate separate affiliated licensees to represent each party. Each designated agent then owes full fiduciary duties to their assigned client, preserving advocacy for both sides.

questions.question_text: Agent Kim is designated to represent the seller in an in-house transaction. What level of duty does Kim owe to the seller?
distractors: Limited duties of honesty only
Transaction broker duties
Reduced duties shared with the buyer
questions.explanation: In Kansas designated agency, only the designated agent has the duties of a seller's agent. Under K.S.A. 58-30,106, Kim must promote the seller's interests with utmost good faith, loyalty and fidelity, present all offers, keep the seller's confidences, account for funds, and disclose known adverse material facts.

questions.question_text: Two agents in the same firm are designated to represent opposite parties. The buyer's designated agent learns the buyer will pay above asking price. With whom is the agent prohibited from sharing this information?
questions.correct_answer: The seller's designated agent in the firm
distractors: The supervising broker, when seeking advice
The buyer's lender, at the buyer's request
The buyer's attorney, at the buyer's direction
questions.explanation: Confidential information must never reach the other side: the supervising broker, acting as a transaction broker, may not reveal a buyer's willingness to pay more without all parties' consent (K.S.A. 58-30,113(f)). K.S.A. 58-30,109(b)(5) does allow a designated agent to share client confidences with the supervising broker to seek advice or assistance.

questions.question_text: A brokerage employs only one licensee besides the broker. Both parties in a sale are clients of this firm. Can the brokerage use designated agency?
questions.correct_answer: No, two separate licensees are required
distractors: Yes, the broker may designate themselves and the agent
Yes, the single agent serves both with consent
Yes, if the transaction value is under $100,000
questions.explanation: Designated agency requires the broker to appoint separate affiliated licensees for each party. If only one licensee is available besides the broker, the brokerage cannot assign two different designated agents, and must consider an alternative such as transaction brokerage.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes the purpose of designated agency in Kansas?
questions.correct_answer: It preserves full advocacy for each party in-house
distractors: It eliminates the need for written disclosures
It allows the broker to represent both sides
It limits liability for the brokerage firm
questions.explanation: Designated agency exists so that when both parties are clients of the same brokerage, each can still receive full fiduciary representation. Separate licensees are designated to advocate exclusively for their assigned client, preserving the benefits of full agency.

questions.question_text: During a designated agency transaction, the seller's agent asks the supervising broker to pressure the buyer into accepting a counteroffer. What should the broker do?
questions.correct_answer: Decline and remain neutral to both parties
distractors: Assist the seller's agent as the firm's client
Contact the buyer's agent to relay the request
Present the counteroffer directly to the buyer
questions.explanation: In a designated agency situation, the supervising broker must remain neutral and cannot advocate for either party. Pressuring the buyer would violate the broker's obligation of neutrality and compromise the integrity of the designated agency arrangement.

questions.question_text: The buyer's designated agent accidentally shares confidential client information with the supervising broker. What is the broker's obligation?
questions.correct_answer: Keep the information confidential from both sides
distractors: Share it with the seller's agent for fairness
Disclose it to KREC within 48 hours
Inform both designated agents immediately
questions.explanation: The supervising broker must ensure confidential information is not shared between designated agents. If the broker inadvertently receives confidential information, they must keep it confidential and not pass it to the other designated agent or use it to benefit either party.

questions.question_text: In a Kansas designated agency arrangement, which role does the supervising broker assume?
questions.correct_answer: A transaction broker assisting both parties
distractors: A dual agent representing both clients equally
An advocate for the party who listed first
A designated agent for the firm's first client
questions.explanation: Under K.S.A. 58-30,109(b)(4), in any transaction involving a designated agent the supervising broker acts as a transaction broker, assisting both parties without advocating for either. If both designated agents work under the same branch broker, the branch broker assumes the transaction broker role instead.

questions.question_text: Agent Tran tells her supervising broker that the seller will accept $15,000 less than list price. The broker shares this with the buyer's designated agent. What violation occurred?
questions.correct_answer: The broker improperly disclosed confidential information
distractors: Agent Tran violated advertising regulations
The buyer's agent committed unauthorized practice
The seller's agent breached the brochure delivery rule
questions.explanation: The supervising broker in a designated-agency transaction acts as a transaction broker and may not disclose that a seller will accept less than the list price without the consent of all parties (K.S.A. 58-30,113(f)(2)). Passing the seller's bottom line to the buyer's designated agent breaches that confidentiality obligation.

questions.question_text: Which action by a supervising broker would violate designated agency rules?
questions.correct_answer: Advising the seller to reject a low offer
distractors: Ensuring both agents have required disclosures
Reviewing transaction documents for compliance
Confirming that both parties gave consent
questions.explanation: The supervising broker must remain neutral and cannot advocate for either party. Advising the seller to reject an offer constitutes advocacy on behalf of the seller, which violates the broker's required neutrality in a designated agency arrangement.

questions.question_text: A Kansas brokerage cannot appoint designated agents for an in-house transaction. What alternative may the firm use with both parties' informed consent?
questions.correct_answer: Act as a transaction broker for both parties
distractors: Proceed as a traditional dual agent
Represent only the seller and refer the buyer
Withdraw from the transaction entirely
questions.explanation: Kansas does not use traditional dual agency. If designated agents cannot be appointed, the firm may act as a transaction broker with the informed consent of both parties. Transaction brokerage provides facilitation services without full fiduciary advocacy for either side.

questions.question_text: Which brokerage relationship does Kansas law prohibit in real estate transactions?
distractors: Single agency for buyers only
Designated agency within a brokerage
Transaction brokerage with informed consent
questions.explanation: K.S.A. 58-30,103(a) provides that a licensee shall not act as a dual agent in any transaction. When both parties are clients of the same firm, Kansas instead uses designated agency (separate agents for each party) or transaction brokerage with informed consent.

questions.question_text: Before a brokerage can act as a transaction broker in an in-house sale, what must be obtained from both the buyer and the seller?
distractors: A signed waiver of all fiduciary duties
Approval from the Kansas Real Estate Commission
Written confirmation from each party's attorney
questions.explanation: If no designated agents are appointed, the firm may act as a transaction broker only with the informed consent of both parties. Both the buyer and seller must understand and agree to the reduced level of service before the brokerage can proceed in this capacity.

questions.question_text: A buyer asks their agent why the brokerage is offering transaction brokerage instead of designated agency. Which response is most accurate?
questions.correct_answer: Separate designated agents are unavailable in this case
distractors: Transaction brokerage provides stronger advocacy
Kansas law requires transaction brokerage for all sales
The seller specifically requested reduced agent duties
questions.explanation: Transaction brokerage is used as an alternative when designated agency is not feasible, such as when the brokerage lacks enough licensees to designate separate agents. It is not a stronger form of representation and is not universally required by Kansas law.

questions.question_text: A seller agrees to transaction brokerage but later demands that the firm advocate exclusively for her interests. What should the brokerage do?
questions.correct_answer: Explain that transaction brokers cannot advocate
distractors: Immediately begin advocating for the seller
Convert to dual agency with buyer's consent
File a complaint with KREC about the seller
questions.explanation: A transaction broker facilitates the transaction without advocating for either party. If the seller wants exclusive advocacy, the brokerage should explain the limitations of transaction brokerage and discuss whether a different arrangement, such as referring the seller to another firm, might better serve her needs.
